hero.h1: A payment routing simulator for merchant teams building new PSP integrations
hero.sub: Most PSP sandboxes produce too clean outputs to test real routing logic. This simulator helps returns the kinds of outcomes merchants actually see in production: realistic soft vs hard declines, 3DS behavior, latency spikes, cross-border BIN penalties etc.

section_what.label: What it is
section_what.heading: A realistic test environment for merchant integration and routing logic
section_what.body: The Payment Routing Simulator emulates how different processor types behave when a merchant sends them card transactions. Instead of always returning clean sandbox approvals, it models approval rates, decline codes, latency, 3DS behavior, cross-border gaps, and retry outcomes. Merchant can simulate the behaviour by  across five processor archetypes

section_why.label: Why it matters
section_why.heading: Routing decisions are usually tested in environments that do not reflect production
points: Sandbox behavior that provider models is often too optimistic, and all too often the edge cases appear only when the integration is in production and customers are seeing payment failures. Real mix of soft declines, issuer outages, latency spikes, authentication challenges are examples of such issues which can be tested with the simulator

section_who.label: Who it's for
section_who.heading: Merchant integration teams, QA engineers, solutions teams, and product stakeholders
section_who.body: Primary user: a merchant-side engineer or QA lead who needs to validate routing behavior before launch. Secondary users: PSP solutions engineers demonstrating realistic flows, product managers evaluating routing strategies, and data teams generating synthetic payment outcomes for analytics or ML experiments.

section_how_to_use.label: Try it yourself
section_how_to_use.heading: Two ways to hit the live API
section_how_to_use.option_1_title: Option 1 — rank every acquirer for this transaction profile
section_how_to_use.option_1_desc: Use /compare when you want every acquirer ranked against the same transaction profile. Returns each archetype with expected approval rate, latency, top decline code, and fee
section_how_to_use.option_2_title: Option 2 — test one specific acquirer archetype
section_how_to_use.option_2_desc: Use /simulate when you want to test a single provider by name. The full list of 11 provider names is returned by GET /providers

section_case_study.label: Re-run the transaction
section_case_study.heading: Re-run a transaction through different routing strategies
section_case_study.body: A built-in replay flow lets teams run the same transaction list through different routing setups and compare what changes: approvals, latency, retry salvage, and fee impact. That makes the simulator useful not just for individual transaction tests, but also for explaining the business effect of a routing policy to non-engineering stakeholders.

section_howbuilt.label: How it works
section_howbuilt.heading: Ten layers from synthetic data to production deployment
section_howbuilt.body: The simulator is decomposed into ten layers, each solving a distinct problem. Every bullet opens in plain English — readable by a Head of Payments — and then adds the technical detail a tech-lead interviewer would probe. Architecture trade-offs and scaling narrative are deferred to a separate brief.
points: Data layer. A synthetic transaction universe of ~106,700 rows × 140 columns spans 5 processor archetypes and 31 countries, generated deterministically with seed=42 so anyone rerunning the generator gets the same bytes. The CSV is validated by a 150-pattern ASSERT gate (all PASS) and a 502-pattern non-contradiction scan (0 contradictions). A one-time script scripts/derive_profiles.py reads the CSV and emits per-archetype aggregates — approval rates, latency percentiles, decline distributions — as YAML. The running service never opens the CSV; only the derived profiles are loaded.

Provider profiles. Each processor is described by a YAML file (11 files total across the 5 archetypes) under payment_router/providers/. A YAML specifies base approval rate, per-country modifiers for Visa/Mastercard/Amex, card-type modifiers (debit, prepaid, commercial), a weighted decline-code distribution, p50/p95/p99 latency, supported currencies, and 3DS challenge/version rates. Adding a provider is a config change — drop a new YAML in the folder, restart, done. No Python edits, no redeploy of the app image.

Simulation engine. The core simulate_transaction() in engine.py computes approval probability as base × country_modifier × card_brand_modifier × card_type_modifier × issuer_modifier × amount_modifier, capped at 0.99. A random draw decides approved vs declined; declines route through a weighted decline-code selector keyed on the provider's per-country distribution. Latency is drawn from a log-normal (mu = ln(p50), sigma = (ln(p95) - mu) / 1.645) with a 1% chance of a p99-tail sample. A Class-A pattern rule chain then replays 35 domain rules per request (MAC codes, MIT, network-token, risk-skip) and can re-draw the outcome if a rule adjusts the approval probability.

State machine. state_machine.py enforces the lifecycle PENDING → AUTHORIZED → CAPTURED → REFUNDED plus AUTHORIZED → VOIDED and PENDING → DECLINED. Invalid transitions — e.g. capturing a declined transaction — raise InvalidTransitionError and the API returns 409 Conflict. Every transition writes a row to the state_transitions table with from_state, to_state, triggered_by, and timestamp, giving a full audit trail. This is the #1 concept interviewers at Stripe/Adyen probe for SE roles.

Idempotency and API-key auth. Duplicate requests are the most load-bearing safety property in payment APIs — a retry must never double-charge. idempotency.py caches responses in Redis under idem:{api_key_id}:{idem_key} with a 24h TTL; a duplicate within the window returns the cached body and never re-runs the simulator. API keys come in pk_test_ / sk_test_ pairs following Stripe's model; secret keys are stored only as SHA-256 hashes. A Redis sliding-window rate limiter enforces 100 req/min per key and 60 req/min per client IP in parallel.

Webhooks. On every state transition, state_machine.transition() enqueues a Celery task (webhooks.py + celery_app.py) that POSTs a JSON payload signed with HMAC-SHA256 to each registered callback. The signature lands in the X-Signature-256 header; merchants verify by hashing the raw body against their shared secret. Failed deliveries retry with exponential backoff at 1 → 2 → 4 → 8 → 16 seconds (up to 5 attempts), and every attempt is logged in the webhook_deliveries table with status code and response body. The API response itself returns immediately — delivery is fire-and-forget.

Event streaming. kafka_producer.py publishes each state transition to the payment.events Kafka topic with the transaction ID as the partition key. Event types are payment.authorized, payment.declined, payment.captured, payment.voided, payment.refunded. This is the producer side only: a 0.5s TCP probe tests reachability and the API continues silently if the broker is down, so Kafka is never on the critical path. The topic is available for any downstream consumer; it is exposed as a seam, not wired into Project 3 (the recovery engine is a sync REST service and does not subscribe).

Routing intelligence endpoint. query_routing_intelligence.py backs the POST /query endpoint: given {country, card_brand, card_type, amount, currency, issuer_country, use_3ds}, it ranks all 11 providers by projected net approval rate using 500 internal Monte Carlo draws per provider and returns recommended/fallback, retry order, p50/p95 latencies, top decline codes, and plain-English reasoning — including cross-border issuer tier penalties. This is the function the Payment Data Chatbot calls as a tool when users ask routing questions. It is the only cross-product integration in the portfolio.

Serving stack. FastAPI + uvicorn on Python 3.12 exposes ~18 endpoints (simulate, compare, route, query, recommend, capture/void/refund, webhooks/register, transactions). Pydantic v2 models validate every request and response. A Click CLI (payment-router simulate, compare, list-providers, route, server) wraps the same engine for scripting. Persistence is SQLAlchemy 2.0 over PostgreSQL (tables: transactions, state_transitions, webhook_deliveries, webhook_configs, api_keys), with tables created on app startup via create_tables(). Redis handles idempotency and the rate-limit sliding window; Celery workers process webhook delivery. docker-compose.yml wires Postgres, Redis, Kafka (KRaft mode, no Zookeeper) and a Celery worker together for one-command local dev.

Deployment. The API runs in Docker on the shared DigitalOcean droplet 209.38.71.25, bound to 127.0.0.1:8090 (never internet-exposed directly). Caddy terminates TLS, rewrites /router/*, and proxies to the container. scripts/deploy.sh is the deploy path: pre-deploy gates run the pytest suite and a CSV pattern validator locally and abort on failure, then tar-over-SSH the source, then docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d --build, then wait for /router/health to return 200 and run an optional 500-sample live compliance check. No CI/CD system, no PyPI publishing, no Terraform — deploy is an idempotent bash script.

right_panel.title: Compare archetypes
right_panel.subtitle: Pick a comparison to see the request and the ranked response the live /router/compare endpoint returns

chips.request: Spanish Visa prepaid at €45 with EU SCA exemption
Idempotency key hit on duplicate /simulate within 3s
US Visa $1,200 corporate card with Level-3 line-item data

0.recommendation: regional-bank-processor — issuer proximity wins for MX domestic debit

1.recommendation: regional-card-specialist — LVP exemption avoids 3DS challenge under €30; domestic routing keeps interchange in-region

2.recommendation: cross-border-fx-specialist — 30 bps lower FX markup wins once corridor volume > $10k/month

3.recommendation: high-risk-or-orchestrator — challenge frictionless via TRA on $2,500 is borderline; expect 8% fallback to OTP

4.salvage: Second-leg authorization on the regional-bank-processor archetype; total round-trip 327 ms, one extra webhook, net approved

5.note: Duplicate request returned cached response; no state transition, no Kafka event, no duplicate webhook

6.recommendation: L3 capture saves 50 bps of interchange; confirm all line items pass edit checks to avoid downgrade
